data.title: Coinbase, AppLovin, and Other Stocks Could Be Added to the S&P 500 Today — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

Coinbase Global, Robinhood Markets, and AppLovin are among the contenders that could be added to the S&P 500 as part of a quarterly reshuffling of the index that's set to occur after the markets close today.

S&P Dow Jones Indices makes additions and deletions to the index, which tracks the 500 largest publicly listed companies by market capitalization, on a quarterly basis. The changes will take effect after the close on March 21; they're generally announced two weeks in advance.

Barclays analysts have identified seven companies within their coverage as potential candidates for S&P 500 inclusion. The largest is Interactive Brokers, a Connecticut-based brokerage firm with a market capitalization of $80.7 billion.

Coinbase and Ares Management are next on the list, with $54.4 billion and $50 billion market caps, respectively. Other possible candidates include Robinhood, LPL Financial, Blue Owl Capital, and Tradeweb Markets.

Though not mentioned in the Barclays report, investors have their eyes on AppLovin. With a market cap of $88.3 billion, the mobile technology company is the largest of all candidates that meet the S&P Dow Jones Indices criteria, according to Dow Jones Market Data. Another strong contender is DoorDash, with a market cap of $75.7 billion.

As of Thursday's close, FMC, Celanese, and Caesars Entertainment were among the lowest in market cap in the S&P 500, according to Dow Jones Market Data, putting them at risk of removal.

S&P Global announced the addition of Apollo Global Management and Workday to the S&P 500 index last quarter, replacing Qorvo and Amentum Holdings, which were moved to the S&P SmallCap 600.

A company must meet a lengthy list of criteria to be considered. For starters, it must be U.S.-domiciled and listed on one of 10 eligible U.S. exchanges, excluding the OTC Bulletin Board and pink sheets.

Moreover, its market cap must be greater than $14.5 billion, with the primary listing's float greater than 50% of the market cap. The company's sum of GAAP net income over the four most recent consecutive quarters must also be positive, as well as the most recent quarter.

Analysts led by Benjamin Budish noted that stocks selected for index inclusion typically see gains of 7% or more in the period ahead of their first day of trading in the index, "making Friday's event a potential catalyst for the stocks' activity."

However, the stocks sell off by an average of 1.3% in the first week following index inclusion and are down around 1% in the following four weeks, the analysts wrote.

In addition, stocks selected for addition have sold off on average by 16 base points from the announcement to the close of the rebalance day, "and have also seen slightly negative returns in the first day and week following the rebalance," the analysts wrote.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

data.title: South Africa rejects Musk claim Starlink can't operate there because he's not Black
data.description:
South Africa on Friday rejected a claim by multibillionaire Elon Musk that his Starlink satellite company could not operate in the country because he is not Black, and its telecoms regulator said Starlink had not applied for a licence.

In his latest rebuke of the country where he was born and went to school, Musk wrote on X, which he also owns: "Starlink is not allowed to operate in South Africa, because I'm not black".

Clayson Monyela, a senior official at the foreign affairs department, responded emphatically on the social media platform.

"Sir, that's NOT true & you know it! It's got nothing to do with your skin colour. Starlink is welcome to operate in South Africa provided there's compliance with local laws," Monyela wrote. "This is a global international trade & investment principle."

Musk appeared to be taking a swipe at local Black Economic Empowerment rules that foreign-owned telecommunications licensees sell 30% of the equity in their local subsidiaries to historically disadvantaged groups.

South African technology news website TechCentral has reported that Starlink's parent company SpaceX wrote to telecommunications regulator ICASA telling it that it should rethink the 30% ownership requirement for licensees.

An ICASA spokesperson said without elaborating: "ICASA has not received any application from Starlink or SpaceX."

Communications minister Solly Malatsi did not answer a phone call seeking comment.

TechCentral has reported that Malatsi has asked ICASA to consider "equity equivalents" like skills development to allow companies like SpaceX to operate locally.

Starlink operates in many African countries, but in addition to South Africa it has faced obstacles in places like Cameroon and Namibia due to licensing disputes.

Musk attended school in the South African capital Pretoria before emigrating to the United States, where he is now a top adviser to U.S. President Donald Trump as well as the world's richest person.

Within weeks of taking office Trump has suspended U.S. aid to South Africa over its land reform policies and its genocide case against Washington's ally Israel at the World Court.

data.title: Canada Unemployment Rate Steady at 6.6% in February — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Snowstorms and tariff uncertainty left Canada's job market at a standstill last month, with hiring stalling and the unemployment rate holding steady below its recent peak.

If not for the salvo of tariffs launched by the U.S., the static employment picture suggests the odds lean toward the central bank pausing its aggressive cycle of interest rate cuts next week, particularly in light of recent signs of an economic pickup and a slight acceleration in inflation. Instead, economists expect data will start showing signs of stresses brought on by escalating trade threats, leaving the door open for borrowing costs to keep falling.

Employers in the country added just 1,100 jobs in February, after filling roughly 210,000 jobs over the previous three months, Statistics Canada said Friday. That lack of movement came as the country's labor force notched its first meaningful decline since mid-2022 and meant the unemployment rate was unchanged at 6.6%, or 5.6% using U.S. Labor Department methodology.

Economists were expecting about 20,000 jobs to be added to the economy for the month and for the unemployment rate to tick up after edging lower the previous two months from a high of 6.9% in November.

There were signs of softness in the data, though the government agency noted its survey was conducted when severe winter storms buried parts of central and eastern Canada.

Average hours worked were down by the most since April 2022, declining 1.3% from the month before. Statistics Canada said 429,000 employees lost work hours in February, more than four times higher than the average time lost due to weather for the same month over the previous five years.

The snow may also have contributed to dampened labor market participation last month. The proportion of the working-age population who were either employed or unemployed slipped 0.2 percentage point to 65.3%, the first decline since last September as a marginal rise in participation among core-age workers was countered by falls for young workers and those 55 and older.

Still, President Trump's intensified focus on trade with Canada and threats of tariffs since taking office are already weighing on measures of business confidence and may be showing in hiring.

Nathan Janzen, assistant chief economist at Royal Bank of Canada, said that on an industry basis hiring in the goods sector ticked lower in February, including a pull-back in employment for the heavily trade-exposed manufacturing sector that partially retraced a surge in jobs during the prior two months. Employment in transportation services, which is also heavily trade-exposed, fell by about 23,000 but saw an offset from an about 51,000 jump in jobs in more domestically-focused wholesale and retail jobs.

The job market had shown signs of firming in recent months, but economists worry businesses will be reluctant to add to their ranks with the uncertainty of a trade war with the U.S. and could begin laying off workers if demand in the U.S., Canada's biggest export market by far, slumps.

Offering a possible hint at hesitancy, Canadian job postings on Indeed.com dipped in February after rising in December and January.

The Bank of Canada has lowered its policy interest rate six times since June as inflation has decelerated back toward its 2% target, but at the last meeting in late-January it left out any guidance on the path of rates given the uncertainty of U.S. tariffs. Analysts widely expect another cut Wednesday after Trump pushed ahead with tariffs on imports from Canada earlier this week, even if he days later paused levies on some goods.

Tariffs on Canadian exports, and Ottawa's retaliatory measures aimed at the U.S., are expected to push up prices in both countries and drag heavily on Canada's growth prospects. Even beyond the tariff threat, federal government efforts to slow immigration that has greatly swelled the population in recent years is expected to affect the job market in Canada, and potentially soften economic growth that was stronger than expected in the last quarter of 2024 and into January.

Growth in Canada's working-age population in February was less than half that recorded 12 months earlier and the lowest since April 2022, and the labor force in the country shrank by 16,800, the first meaningful drop since mid-2022."Whether or not February's stall in hiring is the first crack caused by tariff uncertainty or simply the product of data volatiity, it is likely that further weakness will be seen in the months ahead due to those ongoing trade tensions," said Andrew Grantham, senior economist at CIBC Capital Markets.

Grantham, like other economists, expects the central bank will lower its benchmark rate a further quarter percentage point Wednesday. "While the Bank of Canada can't solve the tariff issue with lower interest rates, it can help the economy transition towards other growth drivers."

Statistics Canada's labor force survey showed overall job gains last month came from part-time work, with employment rising 20,800 to offset a 19,700 decline in full-time employment. The private sector drove gains, while employment in the public sector and among the self employed was little changed.

"Looking through the monthly wobbles, it's reasonably clear that the job market had been turning the corner in recent months...until the trade war erupted," Bank of Montreal's chief economist Douglas Porter said.

Write to Robb M. Stewart robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: Baidu's $2 Billion Bond Gamble: A Genius Move or Desperation Play?
data.description:
Baidu is making a bold financial move, tapping into the market's appetite for Chinese tech with a $2 billion zero-coupon exchangeable bond offering. The bonds, set to mature in 2032, are linked to shares of Trip.com Group and structured to attract institutional investors through offshore transactions. With proceeds earmarked for debt repayment, interest payments, and general corporate use, this marks Baidu's second major capital-raising effort in recent weeksfollowing a $1.4 billion bond sale outside mainland China. The bonds will be listed on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange's Open Market, and major players like JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley, Goldman Sachs, and Bank of America are handling the deal.

The timing is strategic. Chinese tech stocks have been riding a wave of optimism, buoyed by Beijing's pledge to support AI and advanced manufacturing. But Baidu's stock hasn't quite kept paceup just 12.5% this year, far behind the Hang Seng Tech Index's 35% surge. The company is also contending with challenges in its core business, reporting a third straight quarterly revenue drop as competition in search and AI intensifies. Meanwhile, other Chinese giants are seizing the momentBYD (BYDDF) just pulled off a massive $5.6 billion share sale, the biggest in Hong Kong in nearly four years.

For investors, the structure of Baidu's bonds adds another layer of intrigue. The exchangeable feature allows hedging strategies that could shake up Trip.com's stock in the short term. Baidu, still a major shareholder in Trip.com, appears to be unlocking liquidity while navigating shifting market dynamics. Whether this financial play strengthens Baidu's long-term position or simply buys it time in a fiercely competitive tech landscape remains an open question.

data.title: Catalyst Media Says SIS Extends Audio, Visual Distribution Deal with Racecourse Media
data.description:
Media company Catalyst Media Group's 20.54%-owned Sports Information Services, or SIS, secured an extension to its distribution agreement with Racecourse Media Group by two years and nine months.

Under the deal, Sports Information Services was granted rights to provide audio-visual and data services for licensed betting offices across the UK and Ireland covering British racecourses owned by Racecourse Media, according to a Friday release.

The extension was awarded through a competitive process and will run from April 1, 2026, to Dec. 31, 2028.

data.title: ICE Canola Climbing Higher at Midday Friday
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--ICE Futures canola contracts were stronger at midday Friday, as gains in outside markets provided support. However, activity was choppy amid ongoing uncertainty over United States trade policies.

The U.S. put a pause on some Canadian and Mexican import tariffs on Thursday, but the fickle nature of the U.S. strategy kept markets on edge.

"These markets are very hard to trade," said a trader, noting that the constantly shifting tariff news over the past week made it difficult to get a sense of the market direction. He cautioned that while canola prices were climbing higher at midday, any offhand comments from the U.S. administration could easily see the price direction shift once again.

Malaysian palm oil and Chicago soyoil futures were both higher on the day, providing spillover support for canola. Weakness in the Canadian dollar also underpinned the market.

The rally in canola brought values back above some key chart levels, which contributed to the firmer tone.

An estimated 22,600 canola contracts traded as of 11:45 EST.

data.description: CME live cattle futures return to climbing after a quick breather through yesterday's trading session. data.title: Crypto Executives Descend on D.C. For White House Summit — WSJ
data.description:
By Vicky Ge Huang

About two dozen crypto executives are descending on Washington D.C. today to attend the first-ever White House crypto summit.

President Donald Trump will host high-profile industry players, including Coinbase Global CEO Brian Armstrong, MicroStrategy CEO Michael Saylor and Ripple CEO Brad Garlinghouse.

Trump's artificial intelligence and crypto czar David Sacks and executive director of the Presidential Council of Advisers for Digital Assets, Bo Hines, will also be in attendance.

Attendees said they expect the biggest focus of the event to be on the strategic bitcoin reserve. President Trump signed an executive order Thursday that officially established a strategic bitcoin reserve and a stockpile for other cryptocurrencies.

Some executives also anticipate that the summit will touch on legislation for stablecoins, a type of cryptocurrency pegged to the U.S. dollar. They also expect to discuss banks' interactions with the crypto industry and decentralized finance.

Crypto prices are broadly down Friday ahead of the summit, which is expected to start at 1:30 p.m. E.T. Bitcoin, the largest cryptocurrency, recently traded at $87,329, down about 2% from its level 24 hours ago, according to CoinDesk data.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Italian Stocks End Lower
data.description:
The FTSE MIB closed about 0.5% lower at 38,592.8 on Friday, halting a two-day winning streak, in line with its regional peers.

Markets remained nervous about the tariff war started by US President Donald Trump, who has declared the possibility of imposing "large-scale sanctions on the banking sector and duties on Russia, until a ceasefire is reached" in Ukraine.

Meanwhile, European Union leaders reaffirmed their support for Ukraine, pledging increased military aid during an emergency meeting on Thursday.

On the business front, defense company Leonardo underperformed with a 6.4% decline.

Luxury stocks such as Bruno Cuccinelli and Moncler also performed poorly, weighed down by an over 16% plunge in Ferragamo, following its latest financial results that underscored weak sales and an uncertain outlook.

On the upside, Telecom Italia rose 3.8% after the French shareholder Vivendi reiterated its intention to sell its stake.

data.title: JOHN WILEY & SONS, INC. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
John Wiley & Sons, Inc., a global leader in research and education publishing, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of fiscal year 2025. The report highlights significant improvements in financial performance and outlines key operational strategies aimed at strengthening the company's market position in the Research and Learning segments.

Financial Highlights

Total Revenue: $404.6 million, reflecting a decrease from $460.7 million in the prior year period, primarily due to lower sales in the Learning segment and the divestiture of certain businesses.

Operating Income (Loss): $51.8 million, compared to a loss of $46.4 million in the prior year period, indicating improved operational efficiency and cost management.

Net (Loss) Income: $(23.0) million, compared to a net loss of $(113.9) million in the prior year period, showing a significant reduction in net loss.

Basic EPS: $(0.43), compared to $(2.08) in the prior year period, reflecting the improved net income performance.

Diluted EPS: $(0.43), compared to $(2.08) in the prior year period, consistent with the basic EPS improvement.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The company reported revenue from three main segments: Research, Learning, and Held for Sale or Sold. Research revenue was driven by Research Publishing and Research Solutions, with significant contributions from journal subscriptions, open access, and transformational agreements. Learning revenue was divided into Academic and Professional products, with a focus on digital courseware and professional development services.

Research Segment Performance: The Research segment showed strong performance with revenue of $267.5 million for the three months ended January 31, 2025. This segment includes Research Publishing, which provides scientific, technical, medical, and scholarly journals, and Research Solutions, which offers corporate solutions and publishing services.

Learning Segment Performance: The Learning segment generated $137.1 million in revenue for the three months ended January 31, 2025. This segment includes Academic products, such as digital books and courseware, and Professional products, which offer learning and development services for businesses.

Held for Sale or Sold Segment: This segment reported no revenue for the three months ended January 31, 2025, following the sale of Wiley Edge and CrossKnowledge. These businesses were previously included in this segment and contributed to revenue in prior periods.

Operational Restructuring: The company continued its Global Restructuring Program, which aims to streamline operations and focus on core business areas. This includes reducing real estate occupancy by approximately 35% and eliminating certain positions to align with strategic priorities.

Future Outlook: Management remains focused on strengthening the company's position in the Research and Learning markets. The company plans to continue investing in digital transformation and operational efficiency to drive growth and profitability.

Geographical Performance: While specific geographical performance data was not detailed, the company's global operations in Research and Learning suggest a broad international presence, with products and services distributed worldwide.

SEC Filing: JOHN WILEY & SONS, INC. [ WLY ] - 10-Q - Mar. 07, 2025

data.title: Market Chatter: Textron's Ripsaw 3 Selected for US Army Robotic Combat Vehicle Program
data.description:
Textron's Ripsaw 3 vehicle has been selected as the winner of the US Army's latest Robotic Combat Vehicle competition, Breaking Defense reported Friday, citing three sources familiar with the situation.

Although the Army's official announcement is expected later this month, vendors have already been informed, and sources within the industry and military said Textron was the victor, according to the report.

The Ripsaw 3 met the US Army's requirements for a rugged and versatile unmanned platform, according to previous company statements, but the Army later shifted to a single vehicle size and launched a new competition, the report said.

An Army spokesperson declined to confirm whether a selection had been made, stating that the competition remains ongoing until the awards are finalized, while Textron did not immediately respond to a request from Breaking Defense.

Both the Army and Textron didn't immediately respond to MT Newswires' request for comment.

TXT shares were up 2.2% in recent trading.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Measles cases rise to 30 in New Mexico, state health department says
data.description:
The New Mexico health department reported 30 measles cases in the state on Friday, an increase of 20 cases from its previous count.

All the cases were reported in Lea County, that is located adjacent to Gaines County, Texas, where more than 100 cases and one death in an unvaccinated child have been reported.

The increase in the reported cases comes a day after a deceased resident of New Mexico tested positive for measles, marking the second measles-related death in the United States in more than a decade.

The unvaccinated adult patient did not seek any medical care before death, and the cause of the death was still under investigation by the state medical examiner.

Half of the patients in New Mexico are 18 years of age or older, data from the state health department showed on Friday. Three of the patients are up to 4 years of age and eight patients are aged between 5 and 17 years.

The outbreak, one of the largest the United States has seen in the past decade, has put U.S. Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., a longtime vaccine skeptic, to the test.
